message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Importance</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>As patients with schizophrenia or schizoaffective disorder have a high risk of suicide, and a history of suicide attempt is a strong predictor of suicide, determining whether history of suicide attempt is associated with COVID-19 in patients with schizophrenia or schizoaffective disorder has implications for suicide prevention in this patient population.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objective</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To determine whether a history of suicide attempt is associated with COVID-19 in Veterans with schizophrenia or schizoaffective disorder.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Design</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Cross-sectional analyses of nation-wide electronic health records (EHR).</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Setting</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>United States Veterans Health Administration.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Participants</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Veterans with a diagnosis of schizophrenia or schizoaffective disorder that received treatment at any United States Veterans Affairs Medical Center from January 1, 2020 to January 31, 2021.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Exposure</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>History of suicide attempt.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Main Outcome</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Adjusted and unadjusted odds ratios (ORs) for COVID-19 positivity in suicide attempters relative to non-attempters. Adjusted analyses included age, sex, race, marital status, BMI, and a medical comorbidity score.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>A total of 101,032 Veterans [mean age 56.67 ± 13.13 years; males 91,715 (90.8%)] were included in the analyses. There were 2,703 (2.7%) suicide attempters and 719 (0.7%) patients were positive for COVID-19. There was effect modification by age and BMI in the association of history of suicide attempt with COVID-19 positivity such that the association was only significant in obese (BMI ≥ 30) patients and patients younger than 59 years respectively. In the entire sample, the unadjusted OR for COVID-19 positivity in attempters was 1.42 (95% CI 0.97 to 2.10) and the adjusted odds ratio was 1.90 (95% CI 1.28 to 2.80). In patients younger than 59 years, and in the obese patients respectively, history of suicide attempt was associated with COVID-positive status in unadjusted analyses [OR 3.53 (95% CI 2.10 to 5.94); OR 2.22 (95% CI 1.29 to 3.81)] and adjusted analyses [OR 3.42 (95% CI 2.02 to 5.79); OR 2.85 (95% CI 1.65 to 4.94)].</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions and Relevance</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Young or obese suicide attempters with a diagnosis of schizophrenia or schizoaffective disorders have higher rates of COVID-19 diagnosis; due to possible long-term neuropsychiatric sequelae of infection with SARS-CoV-2, such patients should be monitored closely.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: History of suicide attempts and COVID-19 infection in Veterans with schizophrenia or schizoaffective disorder: effect modification by age and obesity
